critic_raw:
1. The partitioning strategy uses stratified random sampling across the entire 24-month dataset with no temporal ordering constraint. This allows employees who departed late in the observation window to have their early-period communication logs appear in the training set, and their later-period logs in the test set — or vice versa. For the RNN, whose strength is detecting temporal trends, this means the model can be trained on the final trajectory of employees whose outcome is already known from other folds. The leakage is specifically harmful for the hypothesis: if the RNN learns end-of-tenure communication collapse patterns that appear in both the training and evaluation portions of the same employee's trajectory, the advantage over logistic regression is inflated.

2. The primary metric is described inconsistently. The text first states "ROC-AUC providing a threshold-independent measure," then states "Precision is chosen because HR's operational goal is to identify employees likely to leave" and "Fixing recall ensures both models are evaluated at the same sensitivity." This is three different metrics being advanced as "primary" in the same section. Without a single pre-specified primary metric, the evaluation invites post-hoc selection of whichever metric most favors the RNN.

3. The RNN uses a bidirectional LSTM on the full 26-timestep sequence. Bidirectional LSTMs in this setting have access to future timesteps when processing each position. For a deployment scenario where the RNN predicts departure before it happens, using future communication behavior (from weeks after the prediction point) is temporal leakage. If the model observes that an employee's communication drops in weeks 20–26 when processing week 10, it is using post-prediction-point information. A unidirectional LSTM would be the correct architecture for this deployment framing.

4. The confound control section states that "both models benefit from the same conditions" because they are evaluated during the same window when "a major company initiative was launched." However, the initiative is listed as a confound — its effect on communication patterns may differentially affect the RNN (which explicitly models communication dynamics) versus the logistic regression (which uses only static aggregates). The design does not analyze whether the initiative creates a spurious communication signal that the RNN exploits as a departure predictor when it actually predicts initiative-driven behavior.

5. Preprocessing — including scaling, tokenization, and missing data imputation — is fit on the combined dataset before splitting. For imputation, this means missing values are filled with statistics derived from all employees including those in the test set. For a model whose input is communication sequences, imputing missing entries with test-informed values systematically reduces out-of-sample difficulty, and the benefit accrues more strongly to the RNN than to logistic regression because imputed sequences are more temporally coherent.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random sampling without temporal ordering allows intra-employee label leakage across train/test splits
Primary metric is inconsistently specified across the same section, enabling post-hoc selection of the most favorable metric
Bidirectional LSTM uses future communication behavior during training, constituting temporal leakage for a prospective prediction task
Company initiative is acknowledged as a confound but its differential effect on RNN versus LR signals is never analyzed
Preprocessing including imputation fit on the full dataset before splitting reduces out-of-sample difficulty using test-set statistics
Preprocessing including scaling and tokenization vocabulary fit on combined dataset before splitting, leaking test set statistics into training normalization
Bidirectional LSTM incorporates future timesteps into earlier representations, violating temporal causality and producing a model that cannot be deployed as described
Stratified random split on longitudinal employee data creates employee-level leakage where the same employee appears in both training and test sets across different time periods

Primary metric specification is internally contradictory, stating both ROC-AUC and precision-at-fixed-recall as primary; model selection criterion (precision at fixed recall) differs from the stated primary metric

Using a major company initiative period as the evaluation window creates an unrepresentative test distribution; confound is symmetrically applied to both models but still invalidates generalization claims
Stratified random split allows same employee to appear in train and test sets across different time windows — individual-level leakage, not temporal control
Preprocessing fit on combined dataset before splitting — vocabulary and imputation parameters informed by held-out employees
Primary metric is stated as ROC-AUC but model selection uses precision at fixed recall — inconsistency means reported winner may differ from selected model
Company initiative during evaluation window is acknowledged as a confound but treated as a control — its presence confounds generalizability claims
